section.title: Idolaters Condemned
section.sectionRationale: Elders come to Ezekiel seeking a word from the LORD while carrying idols in their own hearts, exposing a hypocrisy far deeper than outward idol worship. God answers their inquiry by naming the idolatry hidden behind their pious posture, then widens the lens to a sobering principle: even the greatest intercessors in Israel's memory, Noah, Daniel, and Job, could not save anyone but themselves from a land under righteous judgment.
section.thesis: God exposes idols set up in the heart as the deepest form of idolatry, refuses to be consulted by those who cling to them, and declares that when judgment justly falls on a land, even the righteousness of Noah, Daniel, and Job could deliver only themselves, not others, a truth that makes the substitutionary righteousness of Christ, who alone can deliver others by His own, all the more astonishing.
section.frameNote: Three movements: elders with idols in their hearts come to inquire of the LORD, and God exposes and answers their hidden idolatry directly; the sobering principle that Noah, Daniel, and Job could rescue only themselves, not their own children, from a land under deserved judgment; and the assurance that a remnant will survive to bring comfort by the very sight of their sobered conduct.

verses.text: Then some of the elders of Israel came and sat down before me.
And the word of the LORD came to me, saying,
“Son of man, these men have set up {{gillulim|idols}} in their hearts and put wicked stumbling blocks before their faces. Should I consult with them in any way?

Therefore speak to them and tell them that this is what the Lord GOD says: ‘When any Israelite sets up idols in his heart and puts a wicked stumbling block before his face, and then comes to the prophet, I the LORD will answer him according to his great idolatry,
so that I may take hold of the hearts of the people of Israel. For because of their idols, they are all estranged from Me.’
Therefore tell the house of Israel that this is what the Lord GOD says: ‘Repent and turn away from your idols; turn your faces away from all your abominations.

For when any Israelite or any foreigner dwelling in Israel separates himself from Me, sets up idols in his heart, and puts a wicked stumbling block before his face, and then comes to the prophet to inquire of Me, I the LORD will answer him Myself.
I will set My face against that man and make him a sign and a proverb; I will cut him off from among My people. Then you will know that I am the LORD.
But if the prophet is enticed to speak a message, then it was I the LORD who enticed him, and I will stretch out My hand against him and destroy him from among My people Israel.
They will bear their punishment, the punishment of the inquirer will be the same as that of the prophet,

in order that the house of Israel may no longer stray from Me and no longer defile themselves with all their transgressions. Then they will be My people and I will be their God, declares the Lord GOD.’”

units.label: Noah, Daniel, and Job
units.note: righteousness that can save only itself

verses.text: And the word of the LORD came to me, saying,
“Son of man, if a land sins against Me by acting unfaithfully, and I stretch out My hand against it to cut off its supply of food, to send famine upon it, and to cut off from it both man and beast,
then even if these three men, {{tsedaqah|Noah, Daniel, and Job}}, were in it, their righteousness could deliver only themselves, declares the Lord GOD.
Or if I send wild beasts through the land to leave it childless and desolate, with no man passing through it for fear of the beasts,
then as surely as I live, declares the Lord GOD, even if these three men were in it, they could not deliver their own sons or daughters. They alone would be delivered, but the land would be desolate.
Or if I bring a sword against that land and say, ‘Let a sword pass through it,’ so that I cut off from it both man and beast,
then as surely as I live, declares the Lord GOD, even if these three men were in it, they could not deliver their own sons or daughters. They alone would be delivered.
Or if I send a plague into that land and pour out My wrath upon it through bloodshed, cutting off from it both man and beast,
then as surely as I live, declares the Lord GOD, even if Noah, Daniel, and Job were in it, they could not deliver their own sons or daughters. Their righteousness could deliver only themselves.

units.label: A remnant left for comfort
units.note: the four dire judgments and the surviving witnesses

verses.text: For this is what the Lord GOD says: ‘How much worse will it be when I send against Jerusalem My four dire judgments: sword, famine, wild beasts, and plague, in order to cut off from it both man and beast?

Yet, behold, some survivors will be left in it, sons and daughters who will be brought out. They will come out to you, and when you see their conduct and actions, you will be comforted regarding the disaster I have brought upon Jerusalem, all that I have brought upon it.
They will bring you consolation when you see their conduct and actions, and you will know that it was not without cause that I have done all these things within it,’ declares the Lord GOD.”

gillulim.gloss: Idols, a term of deliberate contempt related to the word for dung pellets, used almost exclusively by Ezekiel to describe false gods. Here the idols are not physical statues but are 'set up in the heart', showing that outward compliance can hide inward, entrenched idolatry.

tsedaqah.gloss: Righteousness. The righteousness of Noah, Daniel, and Job, three men famous across the ancient world for uprightness and effective intercession, is here declared powerful enough to save only themselves, not even their own children, when judgment justly falls on a land.

basic.context: Elders come to Ezekiel seeking a word from God while secretly carrying idols in their hearts, and God exposes their hypocrisy before answering. He then widens the scope with a sobering principle: even if the three most righteous men Israel remembered, Noah, Daniel, and Job, stood in a land under deserved judgment, their righteousness could rescue only themselves, never anyone else.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a direct, historical confrontation with specific elders and a general theological principle about corporate judgment, not an abstract philosophical exercise. The hardest truth here is double: outward religious behavior can mask deep idolatry, and even the greatest human righteousness has real limits, it cannot be transferred to save someone else.
basic.originalLanguages: These men have set up gillulim, idols, a term of deliberate contempt, in their very hearts, showing that idolatry is fundamentally an inward condition before it is ever an outward statue. Even the tsedaqah, righteousness, of Noah, Daniel, and Job, three legendary examples of uprightness, is declared insufficient to deliver anyone but themselves from a land under God's righteous hand.
basic.typology: The stark limitation that even Noah, Daniel, and Job could deliver only themselves highlights, by deliberate contrast, the uniqueness of Christ, whose righteousness, unlike theirs, genuinely does deliver others, since 'by the one man's obedience the many will be made righteous', Romans 5:19.
basic.christ: Where Noah, Daniel, and Job stand as the ceiling of merely human righteousness, unable to rescue even their own children, Christ is revealed as the one righteous man whose obedience actually does avail for others, Isaiah 53:11, Romans 5:19, 2 Corinthians 5:21; He is the true intercessor these three could only foreshadow, able to save not only Himself but everyone who trusts in Him.
basic.god: God is discerning beyond mere outward appearance, seeing straight through pious postures to idols hidden in the heart; He is also scrupulously just, holding a land accountable for genuine, deserved sin, while still, remarkably, preserving a remnant whose visible transformation will bring comfort even amid deserved judgment.
basic.crossReferences: Ezekiel 18 develops further this principle of individual responsibility before God. Romans 5:19 contrasts the disobedience of one man with the obedience of one man, Christ, that makes many righteous. Isaiah 53:11 says the Righteous One will justify many by bearing their iniquities. 2 Corinthians 5:21 declares He became sin for us so we might become His righteousness.
basic.oneStory: This is the exposure of hidden idolatry and the limit of even the greatest human righteousness, unable to save anyone beyond itself. Every page after this magnifies the wonder that Christ's righteousness breaks that very limit, becoming the one righteousness in history that genuinely does deliver others, not merely Himself, from judgment we justly deserve.

context.literary: The chapter moves from a specific, personal confrontation, elders consulting Ezekiel while harboring idols, into a general, threefold repeated theological argument using Noah, Daniel, and Job as a rhetorical extreme case to establish the principle of individual responsibility before corporate judgment.
context.historical: Addressed to exiled elders who may have hoped Ezekiel's prophetic access to God could somehow secure a favorable outcome for Jerusalem despite ongoing idolatry both there and, apparently, within their own hearts even in exile.
context.canonical: Daniel is likely a contemporary of Ezekiel, already renowned for righteousness even during his lifetime, making his inclusion alongside the ancient figures Noah and Job a striking, immediate reference rather than only historical legend; this chapter anticipates Ezekiel 18's fuller treatment of individual responsibility.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose the hidden idolatry of elders who sought God's word while harboring divided hearts, and to establish, through the extreme example of Noah, Daniel, and Job, that even the greatest righteousness cannot be transferred to save others when judgment falls on a sinning land, underscoring both individual accountability and the desperate need for a righteousness that can save beyond itself.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage describes a specific historical confrontation and states a general theological principle about the limits of human righteousness in the face of corporate judgment; it is not prescribing a ritual for inquiring of prophets. Its enduring truth is that hidden idolatry corrupts even sincere-looking religious practice, and that no human righteousness, however great, can substitute for another's standing before God.

debates.question: Is the Daniel named in verse 14 the same Daniel of the biblical book bearing his name, Ezekiel's approximate contemporary?

views.label: The biblical prophet Daniel
views.case: Some hold this refers to the same Daniel, since he would have already been renowned for righteousness and wisdom in the Babylonian court during Ezekiel's own lifetime, making him a fitting, immediate example alongside the ancient figures Noah and Job.

views.label: A legendary ancient figure named Danel
views.case: Others note the Hebrew spelling here differs slightly from the biblical Daniel and matches a legendary righteous king named Danel known from ancient Near Eastern literature, grouping him more naturally with the ancient, non-Israelite figures Noah and Job.

debates.bearing: Either identification serves the same rhetorical point: even history's or legend's most celebrated examples of righteousness could deliver only themselves, not others, from deserved judgment.

study.originalLanguages: These elders carry gillulim, idols, a term of deliberate contempt, set up in their very hearts, exposing that idolatry is fundamentally inward before it is ever a statue. Even the tsedaqah, righteousness, of Noah, Daniel, and Job, celebrated examples of uprightness, is declared powerful enough to deliver only themselves, never even their own sons or daughters, from a land under God's righteous hand.

typology.type: Noah, Daniel, and Job, whose righteousness delivers only themselves, Ezekiel 14:14,16,18,20
typology.fulfillment: Highlights by contrast the uniqueness of Christ, whose obedience genuinely makes many righteous, Romans 5:19, unlike any merely human righteousness before Him.

typology.type: The surviving remnant whose conduct brings comfort, Ezekiel 14:22-23
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the faithful remnant theme fulfilled in the church, a people whose transformed lives testify to God's justice and mercy even amid a fallen world under judgment.

study.christ: Where Noah, Daniel, and Job stand as the very ceiling of merely human righteousness, unable to rescue even their own children, Christ is revealed as the one righteous man whose obedience actually does avail for others, Isaiah 53:11, Romans 5:19, 2 Corinthians 5:21. He is the true and greater intercessor these three could only foreshadow, able to save not only Himself but everyone who trusts in Him, breaking the very limit this chapter so starkly establishes.
study.god: God is discerning beyond mere outward appearance, seeing straight through pious religious posture to idols hidden in the heart; He is also scrupulously just, holding a sinning land fully accountable, while still preserving a remnant whose visible, sobered conduct will bring genuine comfort even amid deserved judgment.

crossReferences.note: Develops further the principle of individual responsibility introduced in this chapter.
Contrasts the disobedience of one man with the obedience of the one man, Christ, that makes many righteous.
The Righteous One will justify many by bearing their iniquities, unlike Noah, Daniel, or Job.
Christ became sin for us so that we might become the righteousness of God in Him.

study.oneStory: This is the exposure of hidden idolatry and the sobering limit of even the greatest human righteousness, unable to save anyone beyond itself. Every page after this magnifies the wonder that Christ's righteousness breaks precisely this limit, becoming the one righteousness in all of history that genuinely delivers others, not merely Himself, from judgment justly deserved.

sermon.title: Genuine or False Prophets? (Ezek. 13-14)
sermon.why: Carson's exposition spans both chapters 13 and 14, addressing how idols set up in the heart, chapter 14, corrupt even sincere-looking inquiry of God, the same theme of hidden versus genuine spiritual reality that runs through his treatment of false prophecy in chapter 13.

quiz.q: What is the specific hypocrisy God exposes in the elders who come to Ezekiel?

options.text: They have set up idols in their hearts while outwardly coming to inquire of the LORD through His prophet.
options.note: Verse 3 states this exact charge, exposing an inward idolatry hidden behind an outward, pious act.

options.text: They refused to visit Ezekiel at all.
options.note: The elders do come and sit before Ezekiel; the problem is what they carry in their hearts while doing so.

options.text: They openly worshiped idols in the temple courts.
options.note: The idolatry described here is specifically inward, 'in their hearts', not a described public temple scene.

options.text: They demanded that Ezekiel perform miracles to prove his prophetic authority.
options.note: Their sin is hidden idolatry while inquiring of God, not a demand for miraculous proof.

quiz.q: According to verses 12-20, what could the righteousness of Noah, Daniel, and Job accomplish for a sinning land under judgment?

options.text: It could deliver only themselves, not even their own sons or daughters.
options.note: This exact phrase is repeated multiple times across verses 14, 16, 18, and 20.

options.text: It could deliver the entire land from judgment.
options.note: The passage explicitly denies this; their righteousness saves only themselves.

options.text: It could delay judgment for one generation.
options.note: The text speaks of personal deliverance, not a delay of judgment for the land.

options.text: It could deliver their immediate family but not the wider community.
options.note: The text is stronger still: even their own sons and daughters could not be delivered by their righteousness.

quiz.q: What are the four dire judgments named in verse 21?

options.text: Sword, famine, wild beasts, and plague.
options.note: Verse 21 lists exactly these four judgments against Jerusalem.

options.text: Fire, flood, earthquake, and drought.
options.note: These are not the four judgments named in this passage.

options.text: Exile, imprisonment, poverty, and disease.
options.note: These general categories do not match the specific four judgments the text names.

options.text: Sword, famine, exile, and shame.
options.note: Two of these overlap with the actual list, but 'wild beasts' and 'plague' are the accurate remaining judgments, not 'exile' and 'shame'.

quiz.q: What comfort does God promise regarding the survivors who come out of Jerusalem's judgment?

options.text: Their conduct and actions will bring comfort by showing the judgment was not without cause.
options.note: Verses 22-23 state that seeing their conduct will bring consolation and confirm the justice of God's actions.

options.text: They will be exempt from ever explaining what happened.
options.note: The text says the opposite; their visible conduct becomes a testimony to why judgment fell.

options.text: They will immediately rebuild Jerusalem's temple.
options.note: The passage focuses on their conduct providing comfort and confirmation, not a rebuilding project.

options.text: They will be given new names to hide their past.
options.note: No mention of new names is made; the comfort comes from witnessing their sobered conduct.

quiz.q: What theological principle does this chapter establish about righteousness and judgment?

options.text: Human righteousness, however great, cannot be transferred to deliver others from judgment justly deserved by a land.
options.note: The extended argument using Noah, Daniel, and Job establishes exactly this principle.

options.text: Any righteous person automatically protects their entire community from judgment.
options.note: The passage explicitly denies this; righteousness here delivers only the individual.

options.text: God never judges a land as long as one righteous person remains in it.
options.note: The text describes judgment falling even with righteous individuals present; they alone are personally spared.

options.text: Righteousness matters only for religious leaders, not ordinary people.
options.note: The principle applies to individual standing before God generally, not merely to leaders.

questions: The elders sought God's word while secretly harboring idols in their hearts. What might you be carrying in your own heart that quietly competes with genuine devotion to God, even while you look for His guidance?

Even Noah, Daniel, and Job could not save others by their own righteousness. Where might you be relying on someone else's faith or character, a parent, spouse, or mentor, rather than your own relationship with Christ?

God promises that the remnant's visible, sobered conduct will bring comfort even amid deserved judgment. How might your own changed life, after failure or discipline, become a source of comfort and testimony to others?

This chapter shows the real limits of human righteousness contrasted with the limitless righteousness of Christ. How does resting in Christ's righteousness, rather than your own performance, change the way you approach your own failures?
God says He will 'take hold of the hearts' of His people through this confrontation. Where do you need God to take hold of your heart and expose an idol you have not yet named honestly?

translationNotes.note: The original text places a dash-like break describing that the punishment of the inquirer and the prophet will be the same; this has been rendered here with a comma to keep the clause flowing without using a dash.

Scholars debate whether 'Daniel' here refers to the biblical prophet, Ezekiel's approximate contemporary, or an ancient legendary figure named Danel known from other ancient Near Eastern texts; either way the three names together form a proverbial trio of righteousness.

The list of 'four dire judgments', sword, famine, wild beasts, and plague, originally appeared with a dash-style break in the source punctuation; it has been rendered here with a colon and comma to preserve clarity without a dash.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit; four em dashes in the source text, verses 10 and 21, were rendered with commas or a colon per house style. Sermon link verified via live web search and fetch this session, The Gospel Coalition's Carson Center podcast, the same episode covering Ezekiel 13-14 as the previous study.
